clinical_history: Premature ventricular complexes unmask rhythm.  What is it?
diagnosis: Borderline sinus tachycardia is present with a markedly prolonged PR at about 300 ms ("1st degree AV block").  The P waves, partly obscured by the T waves of the preceding sinus beats, are more clearly evident after the premature ventricular complexes (PVCs) -- beats 3 and 9, and the last beat. In addition, there is a left ventricular intraventricular conduction delay (IVCD; QRS duration about 160 ms), borderline left axis deviation, probable left atrial abnormality and non-specific lateral ST-T wave changes. The QT appears long in part because of the superimposed P wave deflection at the terminal portion of the T wave. Note the notching on the upstroke of the S waves in V2-V4, consistent with myocardial scarring with the IVCD ("Cabrera&39;s sign"), in a patient with a history of multiple previous myocardial infarctions. The findings are consistent with  severely impaired left ventricular function.
